teams.name: Belgium
teams.style: Possession-based 4-3-3 with rapid wide transitions through Doku and Trossard; De Bruyne as the No.10, Courtois anchoring a veteran spine.
keyPlayers: Jérémy Doku (Manchester City, LW — most dribbles completed in UEFA qualifying)
teams.form: Unbeaten through UEFA qualifying, then crushed Tunisia 5-0 in their final warm-up. OPENED the World Cup with a frustrating 1-1 draw vs Egypt in Seattle — Emam Ashour struck in the 19th minute and held Belgium for 47 minutes; Lukaku's 66th-minute introduction immediately caused a Mohamed Hany own goal, but Belgium's open-play creation was poor and De Bruyne's influence minimal throughout.
teams.outlook: Outlook darkened after the Egypt draw — Doku was neutralised, the forward line lacked cohesion without Lukaku from the start, and De Bruyne managed the game rather than controlled it. The June 21 duel with an Iran side that also dropped a point is now a must-not-lose. Group G is wide open.

teams.name: Egypt
teams.style: Compact, disciplined low-to-mid block built on the meanest defence in African qualifying, springing counters through Salah and Marmoush.
teams.form: Unbeaten qualifying campaign without conceding. OPENED the World Cup by leading Belgium 1-0 for 47 minutes — Emam Ashour struck in the 19th minute; Lukaku's 66th-minute introduction caused a Mohamed Hany own goal to level at 1-1. Egypt's defensive organisation exceeded all expectations; Salah was contained but a constant threat from the right.
teams.outlook: The MD1 defensive block confirms Hossam Hassan's structure is tournament-ready. Salah has yet to score but showed movement and quality throughout; Emam Ashour's opener proved the team can create without Salah dominating. New Zealand on June 21 is Egypt's clearest path to their first World Cup win in eight attempts.

teams.name: Iran
teams.style: Deep defensive block packed with veteran know-how, direct vertical service to Taremi; historically scores just 0.72 goals per World Cup match.
keyPlayers: Mehdi Taremi (Olympiacos, ST, captain — 59 goals in 103 caps)
teams.form: Cruised through AFC qualifying to a fourth straight finals; preparations overshadowed by a difficult geopolitical backdrop. OPENED the World Cup with a shock 2-2 draw vs New Zealand — Ramin Rezaeian and Mohammad Mohebbi's late header rescued a point after Elijah Just's brace had put the All Whites 2-1 ahead. Iran's historically resolute defensive block failed to appear when it mattered most.
teams.outlook: Under genuine pressure now: dropping a point against the lowest-ranked side in the tournament means Iran MUST beat Belgium on June 21 to stay alive. Taremi was largely anonymous and the defensive shape that won six Asian qualifying points failed against direct running. A crisis of confidence heading into SoFi.

teams.name: New Zealand
teams.style: Organised, physical 5-4-1, direct long balls and set pieces funnelled toward Chris Wood; competitive without the ball, limited creation with it.
keyPlayers: Chris Wood (Nottingham Forest, ST, captain — 45 goals in 88 caps)
teams.form: Dominated OFC qualifying. AT THE WORLD CUP: Elijah Just's brace and Chris Wood's two assists earned a stunning 2-2 draw with Iran at SoFi — Just became the first All White to score twice in a World Cup match; Wood's hold-up play was exceptional, creating both goals by drawing defenders and feeding Just's runs.
teams.outlook: The oracle's most wrong call so far. Wood fit and imposing; Just a clinical finisher; the transition counter-attack was crisp and well-drilled. New Zealand can score against anyone in this group — their June 21 date with Egypt in Vancouver is the group stage's most open fixture. A best-third passage to the knockouts is no longer a dream.

groups.groupReading: The orb shattered in SoFi when Elijah Just's double erased all rankings and left all four sides level on one point apiece — Belgium clung to a draw against Egypt's resolute wall, Iran's storied defensive block crumbled against Chris Wood's hold-up craft, and the crystal refuses to read a clear summit. De Bruyne must reclaim his throne; Salah must score at last; Taremi must channel Iran's historic resistance; Just must prove SoFi was no dream. The second matchday in Los Angeles and Vancouver rewrites everything.

matches.reading: I see crimson waves crashing against a wall of granite that has not cracked in two years — yet Doku's lightning splits stone where Salah's counters only scorch it. The pharaoh strikes once on the break, but the old wizard of Napoli threads the dagger before the Seattle rain settles.
matches.keyFactor: Doku's dribbling vs an Egypt defence that conceded zero in qualifying

matches.reading: A nation playing with heavy hearts beneath hostile skies meets the tournament's humblest pilgrims, and grief becomes fuel. Taremi's single golden touch is all the prophecy requires, for Iran's matches are carved in 1-0 runes.
matches.keyFactor: Taremi's class against the lowest-ranked side in the tournament

matches.reading: The crystal is murkier than it was: Belgium's wide press could not open Egypt's wall and Iran's defensive fortress collapsed against New Zealand's direct running — both sides arrive at SoFi nursing wounds and needing points. Lukaku from the start tips the quality balance, and Doku in Inglewood's noon heat should find the space Iran's back line leaked against Just; but Iran, under pressure and playing with nothing to lose, will break forward where they were cautious with the All Whites. A second Belgian goal seals it, though Taremi may find a first touch of fortune.
matches.keyFactor: Lukaku starting from the first whistle vs an Iran side that must now attack after failing to hold New Zealand

matches.reading: Under Vancouver's closed roof two reinvented sides collide: New Zealand's Elijah Just and Chris Wood — who combined for both SoFi goals against Iran — demand Egypt's centre-backs engage in the duels the All Whites have perfected. Salah, goal-less in the tournament but smouldering, has the quality to find the seams in a New Zealand block that was never asked to defend in MD1 — Wood's side spent the entire Iran game on the attack. Egypt's superior squad depth and Salah's clinical threat give them the edge across ninety minutes, but Wood will test the pharaoh's defenders early and often. Egypt's first World Cup win comes in the final fifteen minutes — but this is no procession.
matches.keyFactor: New Zealand's Chris Wood and Elijah Just counter-attack vs Egypt's Salah-led class — this is the group's most open fixture

matches.reading: Two shields collide and the night grows quiet — this is a duel of locksmiths, not swordsmen, with second place and survival the wager. One moment of Salah sorcery near the death undoes ninety minutes of Persian resistance; Iran must then pray the third-place arithmetic is merciful.
matches.keyFactor: Two elite defensive blocks; one Salah moment decides it

matches.reading: A third-night showdown that will no longer be a dead rubber: New Zealand, who scored twice against Iran, will run Wood and Just at Belgium's back line without the restraint of underdogs. Belgium, whose open-play creation was poor against Egypt, can no longer coast — De Bruyne must perform or the Belgians' World Cup exit from Vancouver is a real scenario. Lukaku's physical presence and Doku's dribbling carry Belgium past Wood's well-organised counter, but the All Whites will not be swept aside.
matches.keyFactor: Belgium needing De Bruyne at his best vs New Zealand's counter-attack that proved it can score at this level

teams.name: Spain
teams.style: Positional-play 4-3-3, suffocating possession and high press; Euro 2024 champions on a 31-match competitive unbeaten run.
keyPlayers: Lamine Yamal (Barcelona, RW — nursing a hamstring, may be managed early)
Rodri (Manchester City, DM, captain, 2024 Ballon d'Or)
teams.form: Qualifying: 5 wins 1 draw, 21 scored, 2 conceded. OPENED the World Cup with a shocking 0-0 draw vs Cape Verde in Atlanta — Lamine Yamal started on the bench (hamstring, came on 71st minute) and couldn't unlock the block; Gavi and Ferran Torres both ineffective; Spain had 27 shots but Vozinha's goalkeeping denied them all in the group stage's biggest upset so far.
teams.outlook: Opta's tournament favourite faces a first crisis: 27 shots and zero goals is alarming. Yamal must start against Saudi Arabia on June 21 and Williams needs to rediscover his Athletic Club best. A comfortable group exit is no longer guaranteed, and the Guadalajara altitude duel with Uruguay now carries potential elimination weight.

teams.name: Cape Verde
teams.style: Aggressive, fearless 4-4-2 mid-block with nothing-to-lose counters; diaspora-built squad with no intention of parking the bus.
teams.form: Historic first qualification — won six of their final seven qualifiers. WORLD CUP DEBUT TRIUMPH: held European champions Spain to a 0-0 draw in Atlanta — Vozinha made save after save against 27 Spanish shots in the group stage's most stunning result to date. The Blue Sharks absorbed pressure for 90 minutes and never flinched.
teams.outlook: No longer just participants — Cape Verde are proven competitors at this level. Vozinha is a genuine tournament-quality goalkeeper. The June 21 test against Uruguay's Bielsa press is the acid test of their defensive organisation; a point or a win would put them in serious contention for second place or a best-third berth.

teams.name: Saudi Arabia
teams.manager: Georgios Donis (appointed April 2026 after Hervé Renard was sacked)
teams.style: Mid-tournament identity crisis: Renard's brave pressing game replaced by pragmatic, counter-based caution; 25 of 26 players from the domestic league.
keyPlayers: Salem Al-Dawsari (Al-Hilal, LW, captain — scorer of the 2022 winner vs Argentina)
teams.form: Coaching upheaval under Donis (appointed April 2026). OPENED the World Cup better than any prediction: drew 1-1 with Uruguay in Miami — Abdulelah Al-Amri opened the scoring in the first half; Maxi Araújo's equaliser denied Saudi a famous result. Al-Dawsari was a constant threat on the left throughout the 90 minutes.
teams.outlook: One point from MD1 reopens the Group H calculus. If Spain are vulnerable (as the 0-0 vs Cape Verde shows), Saudi's June 21 match with them becomes massive — a point there combined with beating Cape Verde could realistically send them through. The discipline under Donis was real and encouraging.

teams.name: Uruguay
teams.style: Bielsa's trademark man-to-man pressing and vertical chaos — a tournament-high 147 high turnovers in CONMEBOL qualifying; the post-Suárez/Cavani era.
keyPlayers: Federico Valverde (Real Madrid, CM — engine and leader)
teams.form: Mixed friendlies, then a 5-1 loss to the USA. OPENED the World Cup with a frustrating 1-1 draw vs Saudi Arabia in Miami — Abdulelah Al-Amri's opening goal shocked Bielsa's press; Maxi Araújo rescued a point but Darwin Núñez failed to convert key chances and the high-turnover system that destroyed Italy in qualifying never clicked.
teams.outlook: Far from convincing in MD1 — Núñez erratic, Valverde worked alone without support. Cape Verde on June 21 is a must-win: Bielsa's press against the same defensive discipline that held Spain to 27 shots poses the group's biggest tactical question. Uruguay's class is not in doubt; their ability to break a structured block is.

groups.groupReading: The glass cracked in Atlanta and Miami on the fifth day: Spain's armada fired twenty-seven shots and Vozinha caught every one; in Miami the desert prince drew first blood and Bielsa's press could not find a second gear. All four sides stand on one point with the group entirely unwritten — the oracle sees Yamal healed and Spain restored, but Vozinha's gloves have stained the crystal's certainty and every second act in this group arrives with genuine consequence.

matches.reading: Under Atlanta's sealed roof the orb shows a thousand passes weaving a net around the brave islanders — courage is no antidote to Rodri's geometry. Pedri conducts, the Blue Sharks chase shadows, and even with Yamal wrapped in cotton wool the champions stroll.
matches.keyFactor: Spain's possession machine vs debutant nerves; climate-controlled venue suits tiki-taka

matches.reading: Miami's humidity tests Bielsa's running men, but a team that changed its general seven weeks before war cannot hide its confusion. Valverde seizes the second half by the throat; Núñez, chaos incarnate, adds the second. No Argentina-2022 lightning strikes twice here.
matches.keyFactor: Saudi coaching turmoil vs Bielsa's high turnovers

matches.reading: Back in the Atlanta sealed roof, and now it is Spain under existential pressure — Rodri's geometry and Pedri's control must unlock what twenty-seven shots could not against Cape Verde's Vozinha. Saudi Arabia drew 1-1 with Uruguay on MD1 and will not simply lie down: Donis's side showed shape and a cutting edge through Al-Dawsari. Yamal, expected to start this time and freed from the hamstring shackles, changes Spain's rhythm in the final third; two strikes find their way past a defence thinner than Vozinha's that was, but the three-goal procession the glass once showed has been washed away by the Cape Verde rain.
matches.keyFactor: Yamal starting for the first time vs a Saudi side with real shape under Donis — Spain need to win but the margin will be earned, not gifted

matches.reading: Bielsa's wolves must hunt against the same defensive wall that absorbed Spain's twenty-seven shots without conceding — and Uruguay know it. Valverde's long-range threat and Núñez's physical menace will ultimately be too much for Vozinha and the Blue Sharks to absorb for ninety minutes, and one moment of Araújo's aerial authority from a set piece breaks the spell; yet Cape Verde, now unbroken at this level, will find one counter-punch through Monteiro or Livramento before Uruguay seals it late. Two strikes for La Celeste, one for the island warriors who leave Miami with their heads held high.
matches.keyFactor: Uruguay's Bielsa press vs the defensive discipline that held Spain to 27 shots — Núñez must convert the chances his MD1 performance squandered

matches.reading: Houston's dome hosts the group's second most consequential night: Cape Verde, who held Spain to nothing, and Saudi Arabia, who took a point off Uruguay, have both shown they can compete. Vozinha's gloves against Al-Dawsari's left foot is the duel that defines it; Cape Verde's defensive organisation under pressure has been the tournament's most pleasant revelation, and the island belief — forged in ninety minutes against the European champions — carries them through a tight game by a single goal. The borrowed general from Athens cannot quite unlock what the boy-king of Madrid could not.
matches.keyFactor: Cape Verde's Vozinha and defensive solidity vs Saudi's Al-Dawsari — a tight, tense qualifier with one goal in it

matches.reading: At 1,500 metres where the air thins and truth arrives early, the match that decides Group H's order meets sides who have both been humbled: Spain's 27-shot zero and Uruguay's failure to beat Saudi Arabia have rewritten the ledger. If both sides arrive with qualification already secured this could be calm; if Spain need a win after the Saudi Arabia game or Uruguay need the points after Cape Verde, the altitude duel becomes a genuine battle. The orb sees Núñez finding his first goal of the tournament and Spain's bench depth equalising — Deschamps said the mountain group is where champions are forged; Deschamps is not here, but the lesson is.
matches.keyFactor: Guadalajara altitude and both sides' unresolved attacking crises — this game's stakes depend entirely on June 21's results

teams.name: France
teams.style: Pragmatic, transition-lethal 4-2-3-1; a tournament-hardened machine that has reached the final in four of the last seven World Cups. Mbappé central, Dembélé wide, Tchouaméni shielding.
keyPlayers: Kylian Mbappé (Real Madrid, ST, captain — 12 goals over the last two World Cups)
Ousmane Dembélé (PSG, FW, 2025 Ballon d'Or winner)
William Saliba (Arsenal, CB — playing through a back issue)
teams.form: Qualified unbeaten, won four of last five, closed warm-ups with a 3-1 win over Northern Ireland. All 26 declared fit.
teams.outlook: Opta: 95.3% to advance, 13% tournament winners (No.2 pick). Drawn into the closest thing to a Group of Death, but the ceiling remains the trophy.

teams.name: Senegal
teams.style: Athletic, physically imposing 4-3-3 with a granite spine and pace in wide areas; Africa's top-ranked side, though chance-creation can run dry against deep blocks.
keyPlayers: Sadio Mané (Al-Nassr, FW, talisman — returns at 34)
teams.form: Turbulent: won AFCON 2025 on the pitch only for the title to be stripped via a forfeit ruling — a wound and a motivation. Warm-ups: 2-3 loss to USA, 0-0 with Saudi Arabia; goals have dried up.
teams.outlook: Opta: 62% to advance. Third straight finals; the 2002 ghost (beating France in the opener) walks beside them, but Norway blocks the clean route to the last 16.

teams.name: Iraq
teams.style: Deep organised block, set-piece menace, channel balls to Aymen Hussein; resolute, limited, emotionally turbocharged at a first finals since 1986.
keyPlayers: Aymen Hussein (ST — 8 goals in AFC qualifying)
teams.form: Beat Bolivia in the March inter-confederation playoff to qualify, then lost their June warm-up to Venezuela; winless in all six previous World Cup matches (1986).
teams.outlook: Opta: 27.1% to advance — second-lowest of all 48 teams. The Lions of Mesopotamia's realistic dream is one historic point; the Senegal finale in Toronto is their best window.

teams.name: Norway
teams.style: Ruthless transition 4-4-2 — sit compact, then release Haaland, Sørloth and Nusa at terrifying speed; averaged 4.6 goals per qualifier, the highest by any European nation ever.
keyPlayers: Erling Haaland (Manchester City, ST — 16 qualifying goals)
Martin Ødegaard (Arsenal, AM, captain — fresh off Arsenal's first title in 22 years)
teams.form: Perfect qualifying: 8 wins from 8, 37-5, including a 7-1 aggregate demolition of Italy. First World Cup since 1998 and the tournament's most feared dark horse.
teams.outlook: Opta: 82.3% to advance, 25.2% to top the group. If Ødegaard stays fit to feed Haaland, Norway can genuinely take France's crown in Foxborough.

groups.groupReading: Here the glass darkens, for this is the death-group of the western draw: the world's elite, a Viking storm averaging four-and-a-half goals a game, and a wounded African champion stripped of its crown by men in suits. The runes say France by a whisker over Haaland's Norway, Senegal scrapping into the third-place lottery, and brave Iraq collecting memories.

matches.venue: MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford (New York/New Jersey)
matches.reading: The spirits whisper of 2002, when Senegal toppled France in an opener — but this French side is no fading champion, and Senegal's well of goals has run dry. Mbappé, hunting the all-time record, strikes either side of Koulibaly's heroic resistance; the ghost stays buried.
matches.keyFactor: Senegal's blunt recent attack vs France's elite transitions

matches.reading: Forty years of waiting end for Iraq beneath a hail of Norwegian iron — sentiment is sweet, but Haaland does not deal in sentiment. The deep block holds for half an hour before Ødegaard's scalpel finds the seam; the giant feasts twice, as he did sixteen times on the road here.
matches.keyFactor: Haaland against the weakest defence in the group

matches.reading: The orb shows brotherly love withheld: Arnold's brave wall against the deepest squad on earth. Dembélé returns dancing, Doué and Olise queue behind him, and Iraq's set-piece prayers go unanswered — France rest legs in the final half hour with the job long done.
matches.keyFactor: France's overwhelming squad depth vs Iraq's winless World Cup history

matches.venue: MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford (New York/New Jersey)
matches.reading: Two storms meet over the Meadowlands — Senegal's wronged warriors, fighting for a stolen crown, against the north wind that buried Italy twice. Sarr and Mané land the first blow, but Sørloth rises at a corner and Haaland, inevitably, has the final word in a match of brutal beauty.
matches.keyFactor: Haaland-Sørloth aerial power vs Senegal's need to chase the game

matches.reading: The summit duel the fates have circled since December: the world's finest against the perfect-record raiders, with first place and a kinder bracket the prize. Haaland scores because he always scores; Mbappé answers because he always answers — and Deschamps, the old fox, will not lose a game he does not need to win.
matches.keyFactor: Both likely already qualified; group winner decided on goal difference

matches.reading: In Toronto the lions of Teranga must roar or go home, and a wounded lion is the most dangerous kind. Mané, in perhaps his last World Cup act, breaks Iraq's resistance before the hour; Ndiaye seals a victory that should be worth a best-third passage into the knockout light.
matches.keyFactor: Senegal's superior athleticism and desperation for goal difference
